Name: stability_aware_layer_routing
Title: Stability-Aware Dynamic Layer Routing: Spectral Perturbation Bounds for Latency-Efficient Inference
Short Hypothesis: Layer-skipping decisions should be conditioned not solely on task difficulty (confidence/entropy) but on the spectral stability of hidden states induced by the skip. By measuring the Jacobian condition number of post-skip activations via single-pass randomized SVD and routing only when stability remains bounded, we can enforce strict PAC-Bayes risk guarantees on accuracy while maintaining latency efficiency. This addresses a critical gap: existing layer-skipping methods treat routing as independent of hidden-state perturbation, ignoring cascading spectral degradation. We hypothesize that stability-aware routing achieves superior throughput-accuracy Pareto frontiers by avoiding high-perturbation skips that degrade downstream layer robustness.
Related Work: Recent layer-skipping methods (DASH, ASTER, ConfLayers, BUDDY, ITT) use MDP-based or confidence-based routing, treating routing decisions as independent of activation geometry. Spectral Quantization Routing (SQR) uses condition numbers for precision allocation but not for layer selection. Prior work on stochastic depth (Huang et al. 2016) trains models to be robust to arbitrary layer skipping but provides no runtime stability diagnostics. Geometric Signal Separation (GSS) uses PCA for noise filtering but not for routing decisions. Our key distinction: we couple layer-routing decisions with real-time Jacobian spectral diagnostics, deriving PAC-Bayes bounds that link condition number thresholds to generalization risk under perturbation. This is the first work to formalize layer-skipping as a constrained optimization problem over stability budgets, not merely confidence thresholds. Unlike MDP-based approaches, our method requires no auxiliary networks, training, or parallel sampling—only amortized single-pass SVD.
Abstract: Dynamic layer-skipping promises significant latency reductions for LLM inference, yet existing methods route based on task difficulty (confidence/entropy) without accounting for the spectral instability introduced by skipping. We hypothesize that layer-skipping decisions should be conditioned on the Jacobian condition number of post-skip hidden states: skipping should only occur when perturbation remains bounded, preventing cascading spectral degradation. We propose Stability-Aware Layer Routing (SALR), a training-free framework that computes the local Jacobian singular-value spectrum of intermediate activations using single-pass randomized SVD and routes layers deterministically based on whether the condition number remains below a calibrated threshold. We derive PAC-Bayes bounds linking condition number thresholds to generalization risk under perturbation, establishing formal accuracy guarantees. SALR enforces strict wall-clock latency budgets by amortizing spectral diagnostics into a per-token overhead budget (≤5% of layer computation). Evaluated on single-GPU setups across Llama-2, Mistral, and GPT-2 on diverse benchmarks (MMLU, GSM8K, WikiText-103), SALR achieves a superior throughput-accuracy Pareto frontier compared to confidence-based (ConfLayers), MDP-based (DASH), and budget-driven (BUDDY) baselines. Notably, SALR achieves 1.8–2.2× speedup with <0.5% accuracy drop, while maintaining explicit stability guarantees absent in prior work. By coupling routing with spectral diagnostics, SALR bridges mechanistic interpretability and system-scale efficiency.

Experiments.name: Experiment 1: Spectral Stability Measurement Under Layer Skipping
Experiments.description: For each model (Llama-2-7B, Mistral-7B, GPT-2-medium), measure the Jacobian condition number κ(J) of hidden states h_i before and after skipping layer i. Compute κ(J) via randomized SVD (power iteration, 2 passes) on the Jacobian matrix ∂h_{i+1}/∂h_i. Measure for 1000 random tokens from MMLU validation set. Hypothesis: κ(J) increases sharply when layer i is skipped; well-conditioned layers (κ < 10) show <5% increase, ill-conditioned layers (κ > 100) show >50% increase. Metric: plot κ(J) vs. layer index for both skip/no-skip cases; compute median and 95th percentile condition numbers. Expected outcome: identify layer-wise stability thresholds that predict downstream robustness.
Experiments.algorithmic_detail: For layer i and token t: (1) Extract h_i (hidden state before layer i) and h_i' (hidden state after layer i). (2) Compute Jacobian approximation J ≈ (h_i' - h_i) / ε for small perturbation ε = 1e-6. (3) Apply randomized SVD to J (rank-100 approximation, 2 passes). (4) Extract singular values σ_1, ..., σ_100. (5) Compute κ = σ_1 / σ_100. (6) Repeat for skip scenario (directly pass h_i to layer i+2). Measure wall-clock overhead per token: target <1ms on A100.
evaluation_metrics: Median condition number κ_med per layer

Experiments.name: Experiment 2: Calibrating Stability Thresholds via PAC-Bayes Risk Bounds
Experiments.description: Derive PAC-Bayes bound linking condition number threshold τ to generalization risk under perturbation. For each layer i, train a logistic regression classifier: P(skip_i) = sigmoid(α · κ(J)_i + β), where κ(J)_i is the condition number before skip. Use validation set (MMLU, 500 examples) to calibrate α, β such that skipping occurs only when κ(J) < τ. Hypothesis: optimal τ ≈ 15–25 (well-conditioned regime). Derive PAC-Bayes bound: with probability 1-δ, risk(τ) ≤ empirical_risk(τ) + O(√(ln(1/δ) / n)). Metric: plot empirical risk vs. τ; identify τ* minimizing risk + stability penalty. Expected outcome: τ* provides formal accuracy guarantee with <0.5% empirical loss.
Experiments.algorithmic_detail: (1) Compute κ(J)_i for all tokens in validation set. (2) For each candidate τ in [5, 50], compute: empirical_risk(τ) = mean(loss_i | κ(J)_i < τ), skip_rate(τ) = P(κ(J)_i < τ). (3) Fit logistic regression: P(skip_i | κ(J)_i) with L2 regularization (λ=0.01). (4) Compute PAC-Bayes confidence radius: R(δ) = √(2 ln(2/δ) / n). (5) Identify τ* = argmin_τ [empirical_risk(τ) + λ · R(δ)]. (6) Validate τ* on held-out test set (MMLU test, 200 examples).

Experiments.name: Experiment 3: Routing Policy and Throughput-Accuracy Pareto Frontier
Experiments.description: Implement SALR routing: for each token, compute κ(J) for all layers; skip layer i if κ(J)_i < τ*. Compare against baselines: (1) ConfLayers (confidence-based), (2) DASH (MDP), (3) BUDDY (budget-driven), (4) oracle layer-skipping (knows optimal skip pattern). Measure: latency (ms/token), accuracy (MMLU, GSM8K, WikiText perplexity), FLOPs. Hypothesis: SALR achieves 1.8–2.2× speedup with <0.5% accuracy drop. Metric: plot throughput-accuracy Pareto frontier; compute area-under-curve (AUC) ratio vs. baselines. Expected outcome: SALR dominates confidence/MDP baselines; approaches oracle.
Experiments.algorithmic_detail: (1) For each test example: (a) Iterate through layers 1 to L. (b) Compute κ(J)_i via randomized SVD (amortized cost <1ms). (c) If κ(J)_i < τ*, skip layer i (set residual connection). (d) Otherwise, execute layer i. (e) Measure total wall-clock time. (2) Repeat for baselines using their native routing mechanisms. (3) Measure accuracy on full test sets. (4) Compute FLOPs via layer execution trace. (5) Plot Pareto frontier: latency vs. accuracy for all methods.
evaluation_metrics: Stability margin (min κ(J) across skipped layers)

Experiments.name: Experiment 4: Robustness to Distribution Shift and Adversarial Perturbations
Experiments.description: Evaluate SALR under distributional shifts: (1) Out-of-distribution (OOD) MMLU variants (different prompt formats, paraphrases). (2) Adversarial perturbations (bounded ℓ_2 noise on input embeddings, ε ∈ [0.01, 0.1]). Hypothesis: SALR's stability-aware routing maintains accuracy under shifts better than baselines. Metric: accuracy drop under shift, stability radius (max ε before accuracy degrades >1%). Expected outcome: SALR achieves 2–5% smaller accuracy drop under OOD shifts compared to ConfLayers.
Experiments.algorithmic_detail: (1) Generate OOD variants: paraphrase MMLU questions via GPT-3.5, sample alternative prompt templates. (2) Apply adversarial perturbations: δ_adv ~ Uniform([-ε, ε]^d), perturb input embeddings x' = x + δ_adv. (3) For each OOD/adversarial example, run SALR and baselines; measure accuracy. (4) Compute stability radius: max ε such that accuracy drop ≤ 1%. (5) Plot accuracy vs. ε for all methods.

Experiments.name: Experiment 5: Ablation and Overhead Accounting
Experiments.description: Validate that overhead is amortized and does not exceed budget. (1) Measure SVD computation time per token across models. (2) Vary SVD rank (10, 50, 100) and measure κ(J) accuracy vs. rank. (3) Measure total end-to-end latency with and without SALR overhead. Hypothesis: SVD overhead ≤5% of total inference time; rank-50 approximation achieves >95% κ(J) accuracy. Metric: overhead (ms/token), κ(J) rank sensitivity, end-to-end latency. Expected outcome: overhead <1ms/token on A100, rank-50 sufficient.
Experiments.algorithmic_detail: (1) Profile randomized SVD: measure wall-clock time for different ranks (10, 50, 100) on hidden states of size [batch_size, hidden_dim]. (2) Compute κ(J) using full SVD (reference) and rank-k approximation; measure error ||κ_full - κ_k|| / κ_full. (3) Measure end-to-end latency with SALR enabled/disabled on full forward pass (100 tokens). (4) Compute overhead ratio: (latency_with_SALR - latency_baseline) / latency_baseline.

Risk Factors and Limitations: **Computational Overhead of Spectral Estimation**: Randomized SVD requires O(d²) FLOPs per token. While amortized, this may exceed latency budgets on edge devices. Mitigation: use rank-constrained approximation (rank-50) and profile overhead carefully on target hardware.

**Condition Number Sensitivity to Numerical Precision**: κ(J) is sensitive to floating-point errors in Jacobian computation. Small perturbations (ε = 1e-6) may amplify noise. Mitigation: use double-precision for Jacobian computation; validate κ(J) estimates via perturbation experiments.

**Limited Theoretical Justification for Threshold Selection**: PAC-Bayes bounds are loose and may not directly predict optimal τ*. The bound may be vacuous for small datasets. Mitigation: validate τ* empirically on held-out validation set; use cross-validation to tune threshold.

**Generalization Across Model Architectures**: Optimal τ* may differ across models (Llama, Mistral, GPT-2). Single threshold may not transfer. Mitigation: calibrate τ* per-model; investigate whether τ* correlates with model depth/width.

**Lack of Comparison to Recent MDP-Based Methods**: DASH and ASTER are very recent (2025); direct comparison code may not be available. Mitigation: implement simplified MDP baseline or use published numbers.

**Single-GPU Constraint**: Experiments limited to single A100. Multi-GPU or distributed inference dynamics may differ. Mitigation: document hardware specifications precisely; note that method is single-pass and does not require synchronization.

**Potential Cascade Failures**: Skipping multiple consecutive layers may compound perturbations. Method assumes independence of skipping decisions. Mitigation: measure correlation of κ(J) across layers; add penalty term for consecutive skips.

**Limited Scope of Benchmarks**: Evaluation on MMLU, GSM8K, WikiText. May not generalize to code generation, vision, or long-context tasks. Mitigation: test on additional benchmarks (HumanEval, BLEU, ImageNet classification) if feasible.

**Overhead Amortization Assumption**: Assumes SVD cost can be amortized across tokens. In batch inference or with varying sequence lengths, overhead may spike. Mitigation: profile on variable batch sizes; measure worst-case latency.
